query: evidence that interferon gamma induces indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase expression in immune cells

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Interferons (IFNs) are proteins that belong to the group of signaling molecules known as cytokines involved in the upregulation of the immune response. Interferons are particularly important in fighting viral infections but also play a vital role in tumor suppression, upregulation of MHC Class 1 and 2, signal transduction, and activation of immune cells, including natural killer cells and macrophages. Interferons can broadly be classified into three main subtypes, interferon-alpha, beta, and gamma, with interferon-alpha and beta belonging to the Type 1 interferon subclass and interferon-gamma belonging to the Type 2 subclass. [11] More recently, there has been the discovery of a third subclass of interferons, namely Type 3, which includes interferon lambda. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Research has shown interferon beta to upregulate and downregulate many interferon-dependent gene transcripts (SOCS 1, C-C motif, CCL1, CCL7, CCL2, JAK2, ISG20, and CCL20 were upregulated, while CXCL6 and IL-8 were downregulated). Interferon beta has also been demonstrated to increase levels of IL-10, IL-23A, and IL-5 while decreasing lymphocyte counts, including T cells, CT cells, Th cells, B cells, and NK cells. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, interferons have significant immunomodulatory effects, with interferon-gamma being the predominant immunomodulatory interferon. Type 1 interferons act as antiviral cytokines. Alpha interferons activate NK cells, which in turn destroy viral and other targets; increase the proliferation of B cells, which secrete antibodies against pathogens; and enhance CD8 T cell response by upregulating the expression of MHC class 1 on surface cells. Type 1 interferons also upregulate IL-12Rb and increase the expression of MHC class II molecules. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon -- Toxicity -- Interferon-gamma
retrieved_docs.content: Interferon-gamma increases the risk of gastrointestinal toxicity, induction of autoimmunity, encephalopathy, acute renal failure, and graft versus host disease following bone marrow transplantation. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: TH1: The response stimulated by the release of IL-12 from dendritic cells and macrophages; secretes IL-2, IFN-gamma, and TNF-beta; inhibited by IL-4 and IL-10; provides defense against intracellular infections and fungi

retrieved_docs.title: Interleukin -- Function -- Interleukin-10 (IL-10)
retrieved_docs.content: Th2 cells produce IL-10. Its principal targets are Th1 cells. It causes inhibition of IL-2 and interferon gamma. It decreases the antigen presentation, and MHC class II expression of dendritic cells, co-stimulatory molecules on macrophages and it also downregulates pathogenic Th17 cell responses. It inhibits IL-12 production by macrophages. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Tapinarof -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Recent investigations have highlighted AhR's immune modulatory potential, suggesting its involvement in various systemic diseases, such as asthma, multiple sclerosis, rheumatoid arthritis, allergic reactions, and more. [19] As a nuclear transcription factor, AhR is dependent on ligand binding for activation, which subsequently alters gene expression when activated. Although tapinarof is an AhR ligand, the nuclear receptor can bind to other diverse ligands. Each ligand may stimulate the receptor to generate a similar but distinctive response. Notable examples of other AhR ligands include tryptophan, tryptamine, norisoboldine, kynurenine, and diindolylmethane. [20] The altered gene regulation and expression pathways can vary depending on the ligand that activates AhR.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: TH2: The response that occurs in the absence of IL-12 and IFN-gamma; promotes systemic antibody driven response; releases IL-4, IL-5, IL-6, IL-10 cytokines

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Function -- Immune Response to Fungi
retrieved_docs.content: The innate immunity to fungi includes defensins and phagocytes. CD4+ T-helper cells are responsible for the adaptive immune response against fungi. Dendritic cells secrete interleukin-12 (IL-12) after ingesting fungi, and IL-12 activates the synthesis of gamma interferon, which activates cell-mediated immunity. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Enkephalin -- Organ Systems Involved -- Immune System
retrieved_docs.content: Met-enkephalin has been shown to have immunomodulatory effects on various cell types. These include the upregulation of CD8+ T cells, inhibiting regulatory T cell activity, stimulating phagocytosis in macrophages, increasing proliferation of CD4+ T-helper 1 cells, and stimulating the natural killer cell response. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: TH0: The initial role of activated CD4 T cells, promotes cell immunity by activating dendritic cells and stimulating lymphocyte growth; releases cytokines IL-2, IL-4, and IFN-gamma; can develop into TH1, TH2, TH17, or other TH cells

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Activated helper T cells begin as TH0 cells and enhance immunity by releasing cytokines such as IL-2, IFN-gamma, and IL-4 to stimulate lymphocyte growth and activate more dendritic cells. If IL-12 is present (released from dendritic cells and macrophages), the TH0 cell will transform into a TH1 cell and releases IL-2, IFN-gamma, and TNF-beta, which play roles in both the cellular and antibody responses. The production of TH1 cells becomes perpetuated by the release of IFN-gamma (also known as macrophage activation factor), which stimulates the further synthesis of IL-12. TH1 cells activate macrophages, natural killer cells, and CD8 cytotoxic T cells, which are important for intracellular infections such as viral illnesses. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biologic Response Modifiers (BRMs) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: CD4-T cells can differentiate into T helper cells Th1 and Th2. Th1 cells participate in cell-mediated inflammation by producing interferon-gamma, TNF, and IL 2 that target intracellular pathogens. Th2 cells participate in humoral immunity that stimulates antibody production by B cells to produce IL-4, IL-5, IL-10, and IL-13 that target extracellular pathogens. These cytokines are involved in the mediation of inflammatory response, infection, autoimmunity, transplant rejection, and GVHD. [12] Biologic response modifiers modulate the immune response by either interfering with the effect of these cytokines, [13] inhibiting the costimulation of T cell activation, [14] depleting/inhibiting B cells. [15] JAK inhibitors target at the intracellular level by blocking the cellular signaling pathway.

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: IFN-γ stimulates the expression of MHC-I proteins on hair follicle cells, revealing previously hidden antigens to T cells. Notably, IL-15 inhibits regulatory T cells. IFN-γ and IL-15 activate target immune cells via the JAK signal transducer and activator of the transcription (JAK-STAT) signaling pathway. As a result, inflammatory cells target the hair follicle matrix epithelium undergoing early cortical differentiation or anagen hair follicles, prematurely pushing them into the catagen or telogen phase.

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Current evidence suggests that the cause of the condition is autoimmune, with a significant genetic contribution, further influenced by unknown environmental factors. Reported triggers include emotional or physical stress, vaccinations, viral infections, and medications. [1] These triggers initiate the loss of immune privilege by inhibiting the production of 2 anti-inflammatory cytokines—transforming growth factor-β (TGF-β) and α-melanocyte–stimulating hormone (α-MSH). In addition, they stimulate the expression of major histocompatibility complex class I (MHC-I) polypeptide-related sequence A (MICA) on hair follicles. Subsequently, natural killer (NK) cells become activated, leading to the secretion of interferon-γ (IFN-γ) and interleukin-15 (IL-15).

retrieved_docs.title: Immunotherapy -- Indications -- Use of Interferon Gamma
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic granulomatous disease Bladder carcinoma Melanoma Chagas disease Lepromatous leprosy HIV/AIDS Cryptococcal meningitis

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1949
retrieved_docs.content: Tolpa and acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin are both classified as immunostimulants, agents that enhance or modulate the activity of the immune system. By acting as immunostimulants, these substances can promote immune cell activation, increase cytokine production, and improve the body’s ability to respond to infections or malignancies, making them relevant in therapeutic strategies aimed at strengthening host defenses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1413
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin is classified as an immunostimulant, indicating its role in enhancing immune system activity. Inosin pranobex functions as an immunological adjuvant and, consistent with its immunostimulatory properties, is also regarded as an immunostimulant that can boost immune responses. Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) is identified as an immunological adjuvant, suggesting it can support immune function, and it is additionally categorized as an anti‑inflammatory steroid, reflecting its capacity to reduce inflammation. Together, these agents illustrate the overlap between immunostimulatory, adjuvant, and anti‑inflammatory mechanisms, highlighting their potential utility in therapeutic strategies aimed at modulating immunity and inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2928
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin and inosin pranobex are both classified as immunostimulants, meaning they belong to a pharmacologic group of agents that enhance or modulate the body’s immune response. By sharing this category, these compounds are recognized for their potential use in supporting immune function in various clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin is classified as an immunostimulant, meaning it belongs to the group of agents that activate or enhance the body’s immune response. As an immunostimulant, acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin can be used to boost immune function in various clinical contexts, supporting the body’s ability to recognize and combat pathogens or abnormal cells.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1600
retrieved_docs.content: Indomethacin is identified as the parent compound for the immunomodulatory drug class, meaning that the immunomodulatory agents have indomethacin as their “has parent” relationship. This connection indicates that immunomodulatory drugs are derived from or chemically related to indomethacin, highlighting a pharmacological lineage that can inform understanding of their mechanisms and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1412
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin is classified as an immunostimulant, placing it within the broader category of immunological factors (imunologické faktory). As an immunostimulant, acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin functions to enhance immune system activity, and its inclusion under immunological factors reflects its role in modulating host defense mechanisms. Understanding this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians recognize acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin’s therapeutic potential within the spectrum of agents that influence immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: Tolpa, acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin and inosin pranobex are all members of the immunostimulant class of agents, which are substances that enhance the activity of the immune system. In addition to its role as an immunostimulant, inosin pranobex functions as an immunological adjuvant (adjuvancia imunologická), meaning it can be used to boost the efficacy of vaccines or other immune‑targeted therapies. Together, these compounds illustrate how specific immunostimulants can also serve as adjuvants to augment immune responses in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2705
retrieved_docs.content: Tolpa and acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin are both classified as immunostimulants, indicating that each of these agents functions to enhance or modulate the immune response. Immunostimulants themselves are a subcategory of the broader group of immunological factors (imunologické faktory), meaning they inherit the general properties of agents that influence immune system activity while possessing specific mechanisms that actively stimulate immune cells. Recognizing Tolpa and acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin as members of this subclass helps clinicians understand their role within the larger framework of immune‑modulating therapies and guides appropriate use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1716
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin, inosin pranobex, and tiloron are all classified as immunostimulants, meaning each agent possesses the ability to enhance the body’s immune response. Recognizing that these three distinct compounds belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians consider them as potential options when immune activation is desired, such as in the management of viral infections or immunodeficiencies. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action despite differences in chemical structure, facilitating a unified approach to selecting immunostimulatory therapy for patients who may benefit from boosted host defenses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1489
retrieved_docs.content: Tolpa and acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin are both classified as immunostimulant agents, and immunostimulants themselves fall within the broader pharmacologic category known as tiloron. This hierarchy indicates that tiloron encompasses a range of immunostimulatory compounds, including the specific agents Tolpa and acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin, highlighting their shared mechanism of enhancing immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1295
retrieved_docs.content: Inosin pranobex and dehydroepiandrosterone are both classified as immunological adjuvants, meaning they function as agents that enhance or modulate the body’s immune response. As immunological adjuvants, these substances are employed to boost immunity in various therapeutic contexts, such as improving vaccine efficacy or supporting immune function in patients with compromised immune systems. Their shared categorization underscores the role of diverse chemical compounds in augmenting immune activity and highlights their relevance in clinical strategies aimed at strengthening host defenses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2706
retrieved_docs.content: Inosin pranobex is classified as an immunological adjuvant, indicating that it functions to enhance the body’s immune response. This categorization reflects its role in modulating immunity and supporting the body’s defense mechanisms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2831
retrieved_docs.content: Inosin pranobex, also known by the abbreviation ISA, is classified as an immunostimulant; it belongs to the broader group of immunostimulants that are used to enhance the body’s immune response in various clinical settings. This connection highlights the role of inosin pranobex/ISA as a therapeutic agent that modulates immunity, which is relevant for the management of viral infections and other conditions where immune activation is desired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1279
retrieved_docs.content: suppresses T‑cell‑driven biosynthetic responses; racemic adrenaline stimulates metabolic pathways that feed biosynthetic cycles; and trisaccharides serve as carbohydrate precursors incorporated into complex biosynthetic reactions. Inhibitory interleukins further regulate biosynthesis by modulating immune‑cell function. Collectively, this integrated network underscores that immunosuppressants are essential tools for controlling interleukin‑mediated immune activation, are often derived from or characterized by specialized biosynthetic mechanisms, and interact with multiple metabolic and biosynthetic pathways—knowledge that assists clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies and understanding the biochemical basis of these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 405
retrieved_docs.content: qualification. Immunostimulants constitute a subset of immunological factors; acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin is classified as an immunostimulant and inosine pranobex (ISA) exemplifies this class. Formulations containing amylase, phosphates, proteases, and the antiviral agent inosine pranobex are described as inverse immunological adjuvants, indicating a related but distinct adjuvant function. Collectively, these interconnections form a unified framework in which enzymes, structural proteins, hormones, pharmacologic agents, microbial factors, and biomarker classifications are systematically qualified by physiological context, biosynthetic status, or deficiency, thereby supporting comprehensive patient assessment, diagnosis, and therapeutic planning.
